1: John is given a measuring rod and told to measure the temple — Ezek 40-48 provides the background (the prophet measuring the restored temple in a vision of eschatological restoration). Zechariah 2:1-5 (an angel measuring Jerusalem, after which God promises to be a wall of fire around it) shows the preserving significance of the measuring act. The temple measured is the community of Christ: 'you yourselves are God's temple and God's Spirit dwells in your midst' (1 Corinthians 3:16). Christ's people are the living temple that God preserves through tribulation.
2: The outer court excluded and given to the nations to trample for forty-two months — the distinction between the measured (preserved) inner sanctuary and the trampled outer court reflects the church's experience: external persecution (the nations trample what is visible), while the inner reality (the living relationship with God) is inviolable. Christ's promise (Matthew 16:18: 'the gates of Hades will not overcome it') is the ground of the inner sanctuary's protection even when the outer court is trampled.
3: 'I will give authority to my two witnesses, and they will prophesy for 1,260 days.' The 'I' is Christ speaking — the one who gave authority to his disciples (Luke 9:1; Revelation 10:19; Matthew 28:18-20) gives authority to the end-time witnesses. The 1,260 days (= 42 months = time, times, half a time) is the tribulation period of Daniel (Daniel 7:25; Revelation 12:7) — the bounded period of witness before the end. Christ commissions his witnesses for the full duration of the tribulation, not for a shortened comfortable period.
4: 'These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.' In Zechariah 4:2-14, the two olive trees supply oil to the menorah through two golden pipes — they are the two anointed ones who stand before the Lord of all the earth. The olive oil is the Spirit; Christ is the anointed one (christos = anointed) who supplies the Spirit to his church's lampstands. The witnesses' prophetic power flows from Christ's anointing, not from their own capacity.
5: Fire from the mouths of the witnesses devours their enemies — Elijah called down fire from heaven (2 Kings 1:10-12); Jeremiah was told 'I will make my words in your mouth a fire' (Jeremiah 5:14). The 'fire from the mouth' is the prophetic word of judgment that Christ spoke to the churches in Revelation 2-3 (the sword from his mouth, Revelation 2:16). The witnesses wield Christ's word, which is the fire that either purifies or judges those who hear it.
6: Power to shut the sky (Elijah typology, 1 Kings 17:1) and to turn water to blood and strike the earth with every plague (Moses typology, Exod 7-12) — the witnesses bear the authority of the great OT prophets. At the Transfiguration, Moses and Elijah appeared with Jesus (Matthew 17:3; Luke 9:30-31), discussing his coming 'exodus' (Luke 9:31: his departure, i.e., his death and resurrection). The two witnesses embody the witness of Moses and Elijah — the Law and the Prophets — fulfilled in and empowered by the one who stood between them.
7: 'When they have completed their testimony, the beast kills them.' The key phrase: 'when they have completed their testimony.' The beast's power is limited to the period after the witnesses' commission is fulfilled. Christ himself said 'no one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord' (John 10:18); his death came only when his hour had come (John 13:1; Revelation 17:1). The witnesses' deaths are similarly within divine control: they can only be killed after finishing what they were commissioned to do. This is completion, not defeat.
8: 'Their bodies will lie in the public square of the great city that is figuratively called Sodom and Egypt — the city where their Lord was crucified.' The text itself identifies the city: 'where their Lord was crucified' = Jerusalem, the city of Jesus's death. The witnesses die where Jesus died; their death is participation in his crucifixion (2 Timothy 2:11-12: 'if we died with him, we will also live with him'). The figural identifications (Sodom for wickedness, Egypt for oppression) apply to Jerusalem as it had become — the city that crucified its Lord becomes the type of all anti-God power.
9: For three and a half days, people from every people, tribe, language, and nation stare at the bodies — an unholy Pentecost in reverse: where the Spirit gathered the nations to understand God's word (Acts 2:5-11), here the nations gather to gloat over its silencing. The three-and-a-half-day period deliberately mirrors the three days of Christ's death: the witnesses lie 'dead' as Christ lay dead, awaiting the same reversal.
10: 'The inhabitants of the earth will gloat over them, celebrate, and exchange gifts with one another.' The world's celebration echoes the response to Christ's crucifixion: the soldiers cast lots for his garments (John 19:23-24), the crowd mocked (Luke 23:35-37), the rulers sneered. But the celebration of the cross was short-lived — the third day upended everything. The witnesses' murderers celebrate as the crowd celebrated the crucifixion, and for the same duration: a moment before the resurrection.
11: 'The breath of life from God entered them, and they stood on their feet.' The resurrection of the witnesses after three and a half days directly echoes Christ's resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:4: 'he was raised on the third day'). The Greek phrase 'breath of life from God' (pneuma zōēs ek tou Theou) echoes Genesis 2:7 (God breathing life into Adam) and Ezekiel 37:10 (the valley of dry bones). The witnesses' resurrection participates in and demonstrates the power of Christ's own resurrection — the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep (1 Corinthians 15:20).
12: 'Come up here! And they went up to heaven in a cloud while their enemies looked on.' The ascension of the witnesses (taken up in a cloud as Christ was taken up in a cloud, Acts 1:9) completes the death-resurrection-ascension pattern. Their enemies watch as Christ's disciples watched his ascension (Acts 1:10-11). The public nature of the vindication — 'while their enemies looked on' — fulfills the promise of ultimate vindication of all who witness for Christ.
13: The earthquake and the survivors 'gave glory to the God of heaven.' The resurrection-ascension of the witnesses produces what the trumpet plagues (Revelation 9:20-21) could not: a response of glorifying God. This is the evangelical power of the resurrection witness — Christ's resurrection was the turning point for the disciples (John 20:28: Thomas's 'My Lord and my God!'); the witnesses' resurrection-ascension produces a similar turning point for some who witness it. The resurrection proclamation achieves what judgment warnings cannot.
14: 'The second woe has passed; the third woe is coming soon.' The narrative marker after the witnesses' ministry is complete signals that their testimony was the content of the second woe — the church's prophetic witness, ending in death and resurrection, is part of the divine judgment sequence. The third woe (the seventh trumpet, Revelation 11:15) is the immediate culmination. The witnesses' death and resurrection are not a detour from God's judicial plan but integral to it.
15: 'The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever.' This is the climactic declaration of Revelation's central message. 'His Christ' (tou Christou autou) — the genitive identifies Jesus as the Messiah of the LORD, the anointed one of Psalms 2:2, 6 ('I have set my King on Zion, my holy mountain'). The kingdom inaugurated at Christ's first coming (Matthew 4:17: 'the kingdom of heaven has come near'), executed through the seals and trumpets, and announced through the witnesses is here proclaimed as finally and fully arrived. The eternal reign of 'his Christ' is the telos of all history.
16: The twenty-four elders fall on their faces and worship God. Their prostration mirrors their prostration in 4:10 and Revelation 5:8 — the elders perpetually acknowledge that all authority is derivative and that ultimate worship belongs to the one on the throne. The 'Lord God Almighty' they thank is the one who shares the throne with the Lamb (Revelation 3:21; Revelation 22:1, 3): their thanksgiving is for the reign that is both the Father's and Christ's, inseparably.
17: 'We give you thanks, Lord God Almighty, because you have taken your great power and have begun to reign.' The aorist 'you have taken your great power and have begun to reign' (ebasileusas) is the enthronement proclamation — not that God's sovereignty newly arrived, but that it has now been fully manifested in history. The same aorist appears in Revelation 19:6. Christ's kingdom was inaugurated at the incarnation, executed at the cross, and is here proclaimed as having fully come — the already-not-yet tension of the NT resolved in the seventh trumpet's announcement.
18: 'The time has come for... rewarding your servants the prophets and your holy people.' The martyrs who cried 'how long?' (Revelation 6:10) receive the answer: the time has come for their vindication. Christ is the judge who rewards his servants (Matthew 25:21: 'well done, good and faithful servant'; 2 Timothy 4:8: 'a crown of righteousness for all who have longed for his appearing'). 'Destroying those who destroy the earth' — the destroyer (Apollyon, Revelation 9:11) is destroyed by the one who is life itself (John 14:6). Destruction is the final enemy of the one who came to give abundant life.
19: God's temple in heaven opened; the ark of his covenant visible. The ark of the covenant was the footstool of God's throne (1 Chronicles 28:2) and the symbol of his covenant presence — lost before the Babylonian exile (or hidden by Jeremiah, 2 Macc Revelation 2:4-8). Its appearance in the heavenly temple signals the fulfillment of the new covenant: Hebrews 8:6 (Christ is the mediator of a better covenant); Revelation 9:11-12 (Christ entered the greater and more perfect tabernacle). The ark that pointed to the presence of God in Israel is now fully revealed — because the new covenant, sealed in Christ's blood (Luke 22:20), has fulfilled what the ark represented. The Sinai theophany formula (lightning, thunder, earthquake) announces the covenant's climactic arrival.
